sentences.text: My name is Leslie Banos.
I was born in Nyirbator, Hungary, which spells N-Y-I-R-B-A-T-O-R, in August the 16th, 1923.
It's the-- that's a small town located on the northeastern part of Hungary, approximately 20 miles of the Romanian border and 30 miles of the present Russian border.
No.
My name was given to me, Laszlo.
When I came to the United States, everybody had a hard time saying, Laszlo.
And then officially, I changed my name to Leslie.
L-A-S-Z-L-O, that's Laszlo.
Too many Hungarians have it, and I didn't want it to have it.
[LAUGHS]
My mother was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she died when I was approximately 10 months old.
And my relatives in Budapest took me, and they raised me.
And they were what they call, Hungarian Evangelikus, which means, like Lutherans, you know, non-practicing Christians, so to speak, you know?
Because they were social democrats as such.
And I was raised by them.
And they were a kind of a people, you know, who believed in equality and-- and the socialist and the equalist system, you know, for people.
So I was raised with the respect to all people, regardless race, wealth, or religion, and because my uncle, he was a head of the steelworkers' union.
He was also the-- a senator for the Social Democratic Party in Hungary.
And he was the editor of the socialist newspaper called, which means, the 'Voice of the People.' And in this milieu, I was raised.
Now, my other uncle, you know, his son had a factory called Vanczak Csobutorgyar, which means, Vanczak-- the name, V-A-N-C-Z-A-K.
That was the name of his name.
And he used that.
He built like a steel manufacturing plant.
He did everything, from hospital beds, to bicycles, and baby-buggies, everything in their factory.
And their location was in Budapest, in Csanyi utca 12.
Csanyi, which is spelled C-S-A-N-Y-I 12.
And that's what their location, where the factory was located.
That consisted of two different , parts which was separated underneath by the sewer system.
And I went to school in Hungary.
And I worked in the factory with them, also.
And the same time, I went to school.
And I was aware, actually, this-- the problem which was facing the world, when I do believe was 1938.
I was sunbathing by the Donau, and I listened to the radio.
And the radio announced, it's a beautiful Sunday, nothing can disturb the peace in the world.
And the same evening, Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia.
And that started the so-called conspiracy of the resistance movement from this day on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remember, as a young man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my aunt hired Czechoslovakian refugees, intellectuals, people who had to fled their country, and they worked in his factory.
They slept in their factory until they find ways, till they can go, to France or whatever they wanted to go, to leave the country, legally or illegally, you know?
Because the Hungarian government would not give them working permit.
In fact, they were sneaked into the country.
And this was my first encounter, you know, by helping and hiding people.
In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1941, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the underground, which was the headquarters in Csepel Island, at the time.
Csepel Island is spelled S-S-- C-- I beg your pardon-- C-S-E-P-E-L.
This island was a home of one of the biggest factories in Hungary, called Weiss Manfred.
And they are manufacturing tanks, ammunition, textiles, and the dive bombers.
And the resistance movement asked me if I would work in the factory there and get the information for daily what they-- what's going on.
They put me in as a welder.
And I worked in this factory as a welder.
And daily, I gave all the information what we needed for the underground.
Yes.
They-- I was--
The factory was taken over, since that's owned by Jewish people.
It was taken over by the government.
And they run the factory.
It was the main ammunition factory for the country, which supported the German war effort.
And they-- through the union, they put me in.
Since they figured out, nobody will-- a young man.
Nobody will suspect a young man that goes around.
And as a welder, I worked every part of the factory, every time they needed.
So I had access to every part of the factory.
So I had the information what they needed.
Daily, I know the-- the product.
I know what they was working on it.
I know what they're planning to work.
Which was-- I don't know.
Whatever they did, the information, I gave them.
But I gave them the information what they asked me to give them.
What exact-- can you be specific?
Can you give me some sort of information?
Like I can tell them, you know, they was working on a Tiger tank.
I can tell them exactly how many was on the-- in the process, what they did with it.
I can remember the ammunition, what they were working on it.

I can tell when-- that-- when they brought over the dive bomber from Germany, you know, the parts and everything and assembled it, right there, in Csepel Island, which information was important, for instance, for the air force to know what to bomb and where to bomb.
You know, then that's the information I was giving to the underground.
Yes,
Yes.
Bela Vanczak.
Bela, B-A-L-A-- B-E-L-A, and Vanczak, just like I-- the factory I-- that Vanczak.
He-- he is-- he owned the factory.
He is the one who owned the factory.
And his-- his father was the Janos Vanczak, who was the head of the Social Democratic Party.
And he was the head of the-- the-- he was a senator and so on, You know
Yes.
The people who raised me was my aunt, Margaret Vanczak, who was a mother-- a-- a-- a sister of my mother, and her husband.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were the people that really raised me.
[SIGHS] Not much.
My father-- I-- I loved my father, very much, you know, because I was-- he was very good to me when we came together.
You know, he came to Budapest in business, quite a few times, you know?
And I had a good relation with my father.
Then vacations, sometime, I went down and spent a vacation down with them.
But I, as such a real, close relations, I did not have with my father, because my stepmother, for some reason, wasn't exactly-- I wasn't exactly her favorite, you know?
And despite the fact that my stepbrother and I, we were very-- we loved each other.
You know, real brothers couldn't love each other than we did, you know?
But it's-- my stepmother did not expect it.
You know, it's a-- in a family, when they have wealth in Europe-- and I don't know how is this here-- it's a jealousy, that since two boys in the family, if somebody dies, who will inherit, you know?
And she felt threatened by me, since I was the eldest.
But my brother and I, we did not felt this way at all, you know, whatsoever, you know?
His name was Pal.
Pal, P-A-apostrophe-L.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I know there is a Jewish connection in your family.
Yes.
Yeah, my father was Jewish.
My mother wasn't Jewish.
And when I was-- when my mother died, my family, non-Jewish family raised me.
And so I lost completely contact with any kind of a religion so to speak.
Because they weren't religious people, at all, you know, really.
So I used Sundays to go to amusement parks and movies.
And this was my church service.
You know, Hopalong Cassidy and-- and-- [LAUGHS] and-- and what they call or-- or the other American movies, they were my church service Sundays.
I spent sometimes five, six movies a day, because they were a part of the family outside of us.
What they were in the entertainment business, so I had a press-- pass for all the movie theaters.
So I just roamed around movie theaters watching movies.
Yes.

When Hitler came to power, I was very much, as a young man, you know, appalled, because of my friends, because I had a lot of Jewish friends, you know, whom they had no idea what my background was, because we were-- you know?
I lived with my aunt and so on.
And they were always telling me, they were afraid what will happen to them, especially when the Hungarian government announced that there's-- they do have a Jewish problem.
When they announced this, they know that something will happen.
And we have a kind of a secret bond, so to speak, between kids.
That's, don't worry, I will help you.
It'll never happen.
It-- this won't happen here.
We Hungarians, we-- you know, we won't do things what Hitler does.
Unfortunately, you know, when the Crystal Night happened, and we heard about it, suddenly, everybody became very serious about it.
Even as a young man, what was going on.
And I-- at the time, I tried to figure out what can I do, as an individual, you know, to help somebody?
Unfortunately, at the time, I can't do nothing except express my displeasure at what was going on.
And suddenly, the Hungarian Nazi party, what they called the Arrow Cross or the Green Cross party, became powerful, with the help of the local Germans.
And-- and-- and the-- and the so-called Volksdeutsche.
You know, the local-- they-- they were people who settled Hungary maybe 300 years before or 200 years before.
And they always remained Germans.
They spoke two languages, Hungarian and German.
They speak almost like the Cajuns, here, in-- in Louisiana.
You know, they speak French, you know, but not with the real French, their own language.
The Volksdeutsche, they call it a Volksdeutsche.
They lived there.
And they supported, naturally, Hitler tremendously.
And I had a lot of friends, you know, at the time, between them, too.
And we had quite a bit of discussions and arguments and everything, you know?
And I tried to convince them that it's no difference between people and so on.
Which is-- as long as they were with me, we were fine.
As soon as left me, you know, they became the same people who was always before, you know.
And they were hating people-- people for no reason at all, except, what I called it, because, by God's grace, they were born the wrong race or the wrong religion according to them.
So in 1938, when I heard this broadcast and when Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia, I felt, you know, that the end will come.
That we have to face the fact.
And I know some people, through the union, because my uncle was involved with the union.
And they were, naturally, anti-Nazis, at least 98% of them, you know.
And they formed-- they said, if we have to, we form a resistance movement, and we do what we can to try to keep the Germans out of here and help the people if anybody get persecuted, you know.
And I joined this group then.
And the first meeting was in Csepel Island, with this group.
Because this was the strongest-- the stronghold of the union because of the factory.
There were 30,000 people worked in this factory at the time.
And a majority of them wasn't unionized, you know.
But there was a part which was unionized.
And I was, because of my background, of my uncle, I was able to get contact with these people.
And we did-- from this day on, we worked everything to try to help the resistance movement and whatever we can do in order to help the Allied effort.
You know, at the time, there was no American involvement.
There was only involvement the French and the English, really.
And we tried to do everything.
And the first thing was when the people came from Czechoslovakia to try to give them a place to work.
Because they had to do something.
You know, they mostly were intellectuals, people who never did manual work.
There were professors.
There were army officers.
And my uncle and aunt gave them jobs in their factory.
And sometimes, they slept there, because there was no place to go.
And some of them stayed there for six months.
Some of them, after a month, was lucky enough to get and leave the country and go to either France or southern parts of France.
Or some of them went to England, you know, in order to join the forces, there, to fight Hitler.
And this was the beginning, you know, the whole.
And we did this.
We took the knowledge of my uncle, who owned a factory, because he was-- how can I explain to you?
You know, he was a chicken a la king.
You know, he was afraid of-- of-- for his life.
He was always worried that what will happen if they find out?
They're going to shoot us.
They're going to shoot us.
And my aunt said, no.
She said, we cannot let people die or people get persecuted just because they-- some people feel they are the wrong nationality or wrong race or wrong religion.
So that's what's actually trained me to do what I did later on.
You know, my influence of my aunt and my-- the rest of my family, you know, really, who do what I had to do in order to help people later on.
And I was very active.
When they put me into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be honest with you, I started everything when I went-- I worked for my uncle at daytime.
In afternoons, I went to school at Technology of Budapest.
And this Technology of Budapest, there was-- they did not expect-- accepted any Jews.
You know, there was no Jews allowed, you know?
And then, naturally, I was like an Lutheran.
You know, I was able to go there.
And I seen the seed already there, you know, of the antisemitism.
You know, it was tremendous, you know.
And then, when the underground asked me that they need somebody who would work in Csepel Island, they have a job open as a welder, which is a perfect cover place.
Because, as a-- as an acetylene welder, you know, at the time, you had to go all over the factory fixing whatever need to be done.
It was a perfect place that I can roam free on the factory, which was like a small city, you know, with 30,000 people, and their own airport, where they tried out the dive bombers.
They put me in.
And I worked there and gave the information for them, whatever it was necessary.
And they, in turn, conveyed the information to the right people.
Now, I like to remind you, also, this.
When you work for intelligence, you know only one contact person.
It's not like a social gathering.
The only social gathering was the first time, when I met the union, all the people.
Afterwards, I just met one person, at all-- all the time.
And this was the only person whom I met.
And he used a code name.
And that's where we met.
And that's where I exchanged all the information, at-- at the time, when I worked at-- in Csepel Island.
And then, in-- as the war progressed, they started to-- to take all the young Jewish men and collecting them in camp, all in a military age group, not the older one, like the draft age, so to speak.
And instead of the army, they put them in-- in work camp in Russia or in the country.
I don't know really.
I know they were in Russia a lot, you know.
And when they finished their jobs or wasn't useful anymore, I heard from many sources-- in fact, I heard from the Hungarian soldier.
He said, after we finish with them, we killed all the Jews.
He said, we don't-- we don't need them anymore.
And what the heck we do with them?
We have to feed them.
So I find out the first horror, you know, of-- of-- of-- of what happened, you know to those people.
And this was done, by the way, by the Hungarian Army.
This wasn't the German Army.
What they did, at that time, I wasn't sure of it, because I just-- I had no contact, at the time, with the German authorities.
It was in 1939, 1940.
Victory.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

And then, as went on, and they took some people, even in menial job, like they-- they get pleasure out of it to taking doctors and attorneys and judges and put them like digging ditches and-- and making-- what they call-- bricks.
Or just to dig ditches one day, cover up the next day, you know.
And I seen many of those people, because they use them, also, as, so to speak, slave laborers in the factory when I was working.
So I seen them quite a bit, you know, what a misuse of people occurred you know, during this time.
And this was going on until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 19-- the end of 1943.
And the end-- and in 1943, I had a meeting with a man, who-- my contact man, who took me over to a man by the name of Mr.
Schweikert.
Now, I hope I spell his name properly, S-C-H-W-E-I-K-E-R-T.
I don't know his first name.
He lived in Rakoczy Strasse in Budapest, Rakoczy utca.
Rakoczy is spelled R-A-K-O-C-Z-Y in-- in Budapest.
I don't remember the address.
And that's when they decided they need someone to get more information, what necessary to them, in order to be more active and more successful what they do in a resistance.
I spoke German.
And they were able to attain a paper of-- by the name of George Nemeth.
And he was about my height, my age.
And they gave me this paper.
And he says, you a Volksdeutsche right now.
We want you, if you agree, to join the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- wasn't thinking.
This was so romantic at the time.
When you're a young man, everything is romantic to you.
So I told him, fine.
I joined the Waffen SS under his name, under George Nemeth.
Then name was of-- the SS brigade was named SS Jagdverband-- Jagdverband Suedost Einsatzgruppe Ungarn.
That was the name of the group.
And we went through all kind of a test.
Yes, ma'am.
